narrative: The Trust Nomination Form is used to make a trust nomination over the policy, while the Revocable Nomination Form is used to make a revocable nomination over the policy.

The policy owner must complete the Trust Nomination Form in full with accurate information in the presence of two adult witnesses who must be at least 21 years old each, and they must not be any of the nominees or the spouse of any of the nominees.
The policy owner may nominate only the policy owner's spouse and/or children using a Trust Nomination Form.

The policy owner may nominate any legal entity, be it an individual, association, or corporation, or the policy owner's spouse and/or children using a Revocable Nomination Form; the nomination of an animal is an invalid nomination.

For a Trust Nomination Form, the policy owner must name a trustee or trustees who are at least 18 years of age each; the trustee can be changed at any time, subject to the prevailing law, and a nominee can also be a trustee. The policy owner can name himself as the trustee, but he cannot receive the policy proceeds or give written consent for revocation of the nomination on behalf of the nominees; only another trustee can do so.
A trustee is not required for a Revocable Nomination Form.

The policy owner must specify the percentage share of the policy proceeds that each nominee is to receive using a Trust Nomination Form, and the total of the percentages as specified for all nominees named must add up to 100% of the policy proceeds.

context: This section discusses the priority of different methods for transferring insurance policy proceeds, specifically comparing revocable nominations, trust nominations, and Wills, and how conflicts between these instruments are resolved.
text:
<context>This section discusses the priority of different methods for transferring insurance policy proceeds, specifically comparing revocable nominations, trust nominations, and Wills, and how conflicts between these instruments are resolved.</context>
# 12. PRIORITY OF NOMINATIONS

12.1 There are various ways in which a policy owner may transfer the proceeds from his insurance policy to another person. These include assigning his policy proceeds, bequeathing them in a Will or using the prescribed nomination forms.

12.2 The nomination framework will specify the priority of nominations vis-à-vis alternative methods of transfer. Nonetheless, owing to the varied circumstances faced by different policy owners, it is possible that conflicts may arise with respect to how the policy owner’s assets are to be disposed of.

12.3 To illustrate, under the framework, revocable nomination forms will be deemed testamentary instruments9 , meaning that they will be recognised as legal instruments with which to disburse the assets of the policy owner. As such, revocable nomination forms will have the same legal standing as Wills. Thus, potential conflict can arise in a situation, where the beneficiary named in a revocable nomination form and that named in a Will are different. However, such a case is unlikely to be prevalent.

12.4 Trust nominations are not relevant in the above example, because policy proceeds from an irrevocably nominated policy will not form part of the assets governed by a Will.

header: A. Conflicts Between Wills And Nominations
context: This chunk discusses the priority of claims between Wills and insurance nominations, particularly in cases of conflict, and how the most recent validly executed instrument takes precedence. It also outlines the specific details required in a Will for it to override a nomination.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the priority of claims between Wills and insurance nominations, particularly in cases of conflict, and how the most recent validly executed instrument takes precedence. It also outlines the specific details required in a Will for it to override a nomination.</context>
# A. Conflicts Between Wills And Nominations

12.5 Where conflicts do occur, the person named as the beneficiary of the relevant insurance policy in the latest, validly executed instrument (be it a nomination form, a Will or any other instrument) will be recognised as the rightful beneficiary. This is because the latest, validly executed instrument would be deemed to best reflect the policy owner’s most current wishes. This also reinforces the aforementioned need to keep track of changes to the nominations, as part of the due diligence process. Doing so would help to minimise uncertainty regarding the validity of the different instruments concerned, and would correspondingly reduce the possibility of legal conflict between beneficiaries named in different instruments.

12.6 Where a policy owner wishes to use a Will to govern the disbursement of his insurance policy proceeds, explicit details of the policy in question must be spelt out in the Will. Examples of such details include the name of the insurer, the policy number, and the amount of policy proceeds to be disbursed to the respective beneficiaries.

12.7 For instance, if a policy owner makes a validly executed nomination on his insurance policy and subsequently draws up a Will, the Will must contain explicit details of his insurance policy as mentioned above in order for the Will to override the nomination form. A Will which merely states that it encompasses “all” the deceased’s property may not be deemed to include the insurance policy in question. As such, the Will may not be considered legally valid in respect of the insurance policy proceeds, and the latest validly executed instrument, i.e. the nomination form, may be recognised instead.

12.8 If the policy owner has first made a Will, and then makes a nomination to his insurance policy, the Will would typically not contain details of the insurance policy. In this case, the nomination form, if validly executed, would be deemed to override the Will in respect of the insurance policy proceeds, since it is the latter of the two instruments.

context: This section defines a Will and explains that it is a legal document outlining the management and distribution of an individual's estate after their death, which can be changed by the testator at any time before their passing.
text:
<context>This section defines a Will and explains that it is a legal document outlining the management and distribution of an individual's estate after their death, which can be changed by the testator at any time before their passing.</context>
# 13. WILLS

13.1 A Will is basically a legal document that is executed by an individual (called the “testator”), which sets out how his estate should be managed and the assets distributed when he should pass away. The Will takes effect only on the death of the testator. He is free to change the Will as and when he likes, and as many times as he wants, up until the point of death.

context: This chunk discusses the purpose and requirements of a Will as a legal document for distributing a deceased person's assets, contrasting it with intestacy laws.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the purpose and requirements of a Will as a legal document for distributing a deceased person's assets, contrasting it with intestacy laws.</context>
# A. Purpose Of A Will

13.2 The purpose of a Will is to see that the distribution of the deceased’s property is done in accordance to his wishes. This may be effected by the deceased during his lifetime through a Will.

13.3 If there is no valid Will, then the distribution of the deceased’s property shall be done in accordance with the provisions of the law, i.e. the Intestate Succession Act 1967 or the Administration of Muslim Law Act 1966.

13.4 Any adult of sound mind can make his own Will. The Will should indicate who the executor of the estate is and who the guardian of the minor children should be in case neither parent survives. It is important that the Will must be witnessed by two persons who are present together, and signed at the same time as the testator.

header: B. Who Can Make A Will?
context: This chunk defines the eligibility criteria for making a Will, including age and mental capacity, and notes specific exceptions for military personnel and mariners. It is part of a larger section discussing Wills as a method of distributing assets.
text:
<context>This chunk defines the eligibility criteria for making a Will, including age and mental capacity, and notes specific exceptions for military personnel and mariners. It is part of a larger section discussing Wills as a method of distributing assets.</context>
# B. Who Can Make A Will?

13.5 Any person who has attained the age of 21 years and with a sound mind can make a Will. Notwithstanding this, any soldier (including a member of an air force) in actual military service, or any mariner or seaman being at sea may do so, even though he is under the age of 21 years. The right of Muslims to make a Will is governed by the Administration of Muslim Law Act 1966.

context: This chunk discusses the necessity of obtaining a grant of probate from the court for an executor to administer a deceased's estate as per the Will, appearing under the section on Wills.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the necessity of obtaining a grant of probate from the court for an executor to administer a deceased's estate as per the Will, appearing under the section on Wills.</context>
# C. Grant Of Probate

13.6 The mere existence of a Will is insufficient for an executor to proceed with the administration of the deceased’s estate. A grant of probate must be obtained from the Court to enable the executor to administer the deceased’s estate, according to the directions as contained in his Will.

context: This chunk is part of the section discussing Wills and their purpose. It defines the role of an executor in administering a deceased person's estate as outlined in their Will.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of the section discussing Wills and their purpose. It defines the role of an executor in administering a deceased person's estate as outlined in their Will.</context>
# D. Who Is An Executor?

13.7 An executor is the person nominated to administer the deceased’s estate according to the Will. If the executor is a female, she is called an executrix. The executor can be a beneficiary under the Will or some disinterested party, e.g. a solicitor, a banker or friends, etc.

context: This section, "Trusts," appears within a larger chapter (Chapter 17) focused on the effects of death on life insurance and various distribution methods. It follows discussions on nomination frameworks, Wills, and precedes a detailed look at the Intestate Succession Act. The purpose of this section is to define trusts and their types, and to explain their benefits in the context of estate planning and asset distribution, particularly as an alternative or complement to Wills and nominations.
text:
<context>This section, "Trusts," appears within a larger chapter (Chapter 17) focused on the effects of death on life insurance and various distribution methods. It follows discussions on nomination frameworks, Wills, and precedes a detailed look at the Intestate Succession Act. The purpose of this section is to define trusts and their types, and to explain their benefits in the context of estate planning and asset distribution, particularly as an alternative or complement to Wills and nominations.</context>
# 14. TRUSTS

14.1 In this section, we will discuss the topic of trusts – what they are, the different types of trusts and the benefits of trusts.

context: This chunk defines what a trust is, outlining the roles of settlor, trustee, and beneficiary, and the legal implications of trust ownership. It falls under Section 14, "TRUSTS," which discusses different types of trusts and their benefits.
text:
<context>This chunk defines what a trust is, outlining the roles of settlor, trustee, and beneficiary, and the legal implications of trust ownership. It falls under Section 14, "TRUSTS," which discusses different types of trusts and their benefits.</context>
# A. What Is A Trust?

14.2 A trust is in essence an agreement between a settlor, the person who settles property into the trust, and the trustee, the person to whom the property is transferred. The trust property would be held by trustees “in trust for” the beneficiaries. The beneficiaries of the trust are, therefore, the people who will receive the benefit of the income and / or capital of the trust fund.

14.3 The agreement between the settlor and the trustee may be oral or written, but for ease of proof, the trust agreement should be a written document.

14.4 A trust arrangement, therefore, allows a duality of ownership: the trustee is the legal owner of the property that has been transferred to him, and the law recognises that in addition to the legal owner, there are beneficial owner(s). The law holds that the interests of the beneficiaries are paramount. Therefore, in exercising their powers, the trustees must take into account the interests of the beneficiaries.

context: This section appears after a discussion of the general concept of trusts and introduces the different categories of trusts that will be explained in more detail.
text:
<context>This section appears after a discussion of the general concept of trusts and introduces the different categories of trusts that will be explained in more detail.</context>
# B. Types Of Trusts

context: This chunk discusses the concept of "express trusts" in the context of life insurance policies, differentiating them from statutory trusts and outlining the requirements for their creation. It falls under the broader section "14. TRUSTS", which explores different types of trusts and their benefits.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the concept of "express trusts" in the context of life insurance policies, differentiating them from statutory trusts and outlining the requirements for their creation. It falls under the broader section "14. TRUSTS", which explores different types of trusts and their benefits.</context>
# B1. Express Trust

14.5 We are using the term “express trusts” in a wide sense to cover situations, where there is an express intention to create a trust over a life policy (which intention is not required for a statutory trust). In strict law, there would be three certainties which are required, namely, certainty of words, certainty of subject matter and certainty of objects. In simple English, the intention to create a trust should be clear, and the beneficiaries should be ascertainable.

14.6 Where documents are used to create a trust, there is little problem. Unlike creating trusts for tangible properties, such as landed properties, one would not usually use a “deed” (i.e. a document under seal) to create a trust over a life policy. A less formal agreement or a letter, or even words would suffice.

context: This chunk is part of Section 14, "TRUSTS," specifically under the subsection "B. Types Of Trusts," detailing "B2. Implied Or Resulting Trust." It explains the circumstances under which trusts may be implied or result in relation to life insurance policies, outlining three scenarios.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of Section 14, "TRUSTS," specifically under the subsection "B. Types Of Trusts," detailing "B2. Implied Or Resulting Trust." It explains the circumstances under which trusts may be implied or result in relation to life insurance policies, outlining three scenarios.</context>
# B2. Implied Or Resulting Trust

14.7 A trust may be implied or may “result” in certain situations with regard to life policies.

14.8 Firstly, owing to the death of the beneficiary, or for some other reason, such as the beneficiary being disqualified to receive the policy moneys, a trust may result for the benefit of the person who has originally constituted the trust, or of his estate.

14.9 Secondly, there may be cases where the policy would provide for the policy moneys to be paid to some person other than the policy owner or his personal representatives, and the payee is regarded as a trustee who is not to benefit personally under the policy. In such a case, the payee will hold the policy moneys on trust for the policy owner or the estate of the policy owner.

14.10 Thirdly, there may be cases where a creditor, such as a bank, takes out a policy on the life of the debtor, and the debtor has agreed to be liable to pay the premiums. The Court may well hold that the policy is deemed to be held in trust for the debtor, subject to a charge in the creditor’s favour for the moneys due to it.

context: This chunk, discussing constructive trusts, is part of a larger section (Section 14) explaining different types of trusts and their benefits, particularly in the context of life insurance policies.
text:
<context>This chunk, discussing constructive trusts, is part of a larger section (Section 14) explaining different types of trusts and their benefits, particularly in the context of life insurance policies.</context>
# B3. Constructive Trusts

14.11 A constructive trust is a trust that arises from the operation of a law where the recipient of life insurance proceeds is to hold the proceeds as trustee and return them to a rightful beneficiary. For example, a policy owner executed a revocable nomination on his whole life policy – with a sum assured $\$ 100K$ - to his church as part of a legacy strategy. At the policy owner’s death, the insurer paid the sum assured to the church. It was then found that the deceased had incurred an estate debt of $\$ 10$ . The estate creditor subsequently sued the estate and won. In this situation, the church will be deemed as a constructive trustee of the sum assured of $\$ 100K$ and will need to return the proceeds to the rightful owner - the estate creditors.

context: This chunk discusses the benefits of trusts, specifically regarding asset distribution, avoiding probate, confidentiality, business succession, and asset protection. It is part of a larger section explaining trusts in detail.
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the benefits of trusts, specifically regarding asset distribution, avoiding probate, confidentiality, business succession, and asset protection. It is part of a larger section explaining trusts in detail.</context>
# C. Benefits Of Trusts

C1. Distribution

14.12 The trust deed provides for the distribution of the assets held by the trustees in accordance with the wishes of the settlor and at the time that he intends the beneficiaries to benefit. The trust is useful, particularly in the case of minor beneficiaries, as the trustees can safeguard the assets for the children till such time as the settlor wishes, or till they are of age.

C2. Avoiding Probate

14.13 Another main benefit of having a trust is that it allows the settlor to plan for the smooth transfer of assets to his next of kin, without going through costly probate procedures, especially if the settlor has assets in various countries registered in his name. This is because if assets, such as real estate and stocks and shares in foreign jurisdictions, are registered in the name of the settlor (whether sole or joint), such shares may be frozen if anything unforeseen happens to the settlor. Grant of probate will have to be applied for. Such grant may have to be resealed in the foreign jurisdiction, before the assets can be transferred to the next of kin or in any way dealt with by them. Not only is this a tedious, time-consuming and possibly expensive procedure, but the assets in those jurisdictions may be subject to estate duty of those jurisdictions.

14.14 Because assets settled into the trust with a corporate trustee are no longer in the name of the individual settlor, but in the name of the corporate trustee, which is an ongoing entity, such assets are not frozen and may be dealt with by the trustees readily. However, testamentary trusts would not give the testator this benefit.

C3. Confidentiality

Settling assets into a trust can also provide confidentiality and will not expose your estate to the public scrutiny that probate procedures will.

C4. Succession

14.16 A trust can also be a good vehicle to plan for the succession to business assets. It can segregate the control of business assets from the derivation of economic benefit, thereby allowing control to be left in the hands

C4. Succession

14.16 A trust can also be a good vehicle to plan for the succession to business assets. It can segregate the control of business assets from the derivation of economic benefit, thereby allowing control to be left in the hands of some, whereas others enjoy the dividends.

C5. Estate Duty

14.17 Depending on how it is structured and the domicile of the settlor and beneficiaries, a trust or company structure may also help to minimise estate duty liability exposure in various jurisdictions.

14.18 In Singapore, estate duty was abolished on 15 February 2008.

C6. Asset Protection

14.19 Depending on how a trust is structured, it could protect the assets of the trust from future creditors. At the very least, it can protect the assets from the creditors of spendthrift children and / or the claims of their spouses.

context: This chunk discusses the advantages of having a trust over a will, particularly concerning probate procedures and the management of assets across different jurisdictions. It appears in the section of the document detailing "Trusts."
text:
<context>This chunk discusses the advantages of having a trust over a will, particularly concerning probate procedures and the management of assets across different jurisdictions. It appears in the section of the document detailing "Trusts."</context>
# D. Trust Versus Will

14.20 Having a Will is better than not having a Will. At least with a Will, the person would have planned for the succession of his estate. The absence of a Will when he dies can be very troublesome. This is because the distribution of his assets may then depend on the laws of the country where he has assets.

14.21 However, a Will does not avoid the problems of probate. Probate is a legal process that allows the next of kin of the deceased to inherit the assets of the deceased.

14.22 When a person dies, his assets are frozen, and the next of kin cannot deal with those assets, unless the Court gives a grant of probate. Before the Court can do so, the next of kin has to file in Court a lot of papers, including details of the deceased’s assets. Only when the Court is satisfied that the papers are in order and estate duty (if any) has been paid will the Court award the grant. If the deceased has assets in many jurisdictions, then this process may have to be repeated in all those jurisdictions. Details of his assets will be open to public scrutiny.

14.23 One of the main benefits of having a trust is that it allows the settlor to plan for the smooth transfer of assets to the next of kin, without having to go through the time-consuming and possibly expensive probate procedures, especially if the settlor has assets in various countries registered in his name.

<context>This chunk introduces the final section of the document, which details how life insurance proceeds are distributed upon the policy owner's death, with a specific focus on the Intestate Succession Act for cases where no Will or nomination exists.</context>
# 15. EFFECTS OF DEATH ON LIFE INSURANCE AND VARIOUS DISTRIBUTION METHODS

15.1 Effects of death on life insurance policy is an important planning consideration that requires careful deliberation. As most of the time, life insurance proceed forms a significant portion of the estate left behind by the deceased. This section will focus on the effects of Intestate Succession Act.

context: This chunk is part of a larger document discussing the Intestate Succession Act 1967 in Singapore, detailing how an estate is distributed if a person dies without a will, and includes tables showing the rules for distribution and future value interest factors.
text:
<context>This chunk is part of a larger document discussing the Intestate Succession Act 1967 in Singapore, detailing how an estate is distributed if a person dies without a will, and includes tables showing the rules for distribution and future value interest factors.</context>
# A. Intestate Succession Act 1967

15.2 If a person should die without a Will, the law will decide to whom the property of the deceased person should go to. For instance, should a person die without a Will, his fiancée will be left with nothing from the deceased person’s estate, since the fiancée does not have any legal right over the fiancé’s estate.

15.3 The Intestate Succession Act 1967 provides rules for the transfer of property. This Act spells out the rules for distributing the deceased's property to the spouse, children and relatives. The distribution rules under the Act are fixed. If the deceased had wanted to vary the proportion of distribution to certain relatives, or to leave his assets to charities or non-relatives, such wishes cannot be honoured, unless it has been specifically drawn up in the Will. In Singapore, the Intestate Succession Act governs the distribution of the deceased estate of a non-Muslim. If a Muslim passes away without a valid will, his estate distribution will be governed by the Administration of Muslim Law Act, the details of which is beyond the scope of this book.

15.4 Intestacy can delay the distribution of the estate to the beneficiary or beneficiaries. This delay can create great difficulty if the deceased's family members need the money for immediate needs. The trauma of losing a loved one can be made less painful if there is a Will detailing how the deceased's wishes are to be carried out.

15.5 The Intestate Succession Act is a statute that makes provision for the distribution of intestate estates. Under this act, the distribution will go according to legitimate family relationships. Please refer to the table below on the rules for distribution.

table narrative: ["Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only their spouse, the entire estate goes to the spouse.", "Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and children, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally among the children, with the children's descendants inheriting if a child has passed away.", "Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only children, the entire estate is divided equally among the children, with the children's descendants inheriting if a child has passed away.", "Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and parents only, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally between the parents.", "Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only parents, the entire estate is divided equally between the parents.", "Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind siblings and their children only, the entire estate is shared equally among the siblings, with the siblings' descendants inheriting if a sibling has passed away.", "Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only grandparents, the entire estate is shared equally between the grandparents.", "Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only uncles and/or aunties, the entire estate is shared equally among the uncles and aunties.", "Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind no spouse, children, parents, siblings, siblings' children, grandparents, uncles, or aunties, the entire estate goes to the government."]

Table 1 Future Value Interest Factors For One Dollar   
FVSS Factor $= ( 1 + 1 ) ^ { n }$ where i = rate and n = periods

dimensions.row: Describes the individuals left behind by the deceased.
dimensions.col: Describes how the deceased's estate is distributed.
dimensions.value: Details the distribution rules for the deceased's estate.

raw_grid: Half to spouse and the other half to bedivided equally among the children. If thechildren of the intestate has passed away,then the portion goes to the children'sdescendants in equal proportions
All to be divided equally among thechildren. If the children of the intestate have passed away, then the portion goesto the children's descendants in equalproportions
Half to spouse and the other half to bedivided equally between the parents
All to be shared equally among siblings. If any sibling has already passed away, thenthe portion goestothesibling'sdescendants in equal proportions
All to be shared equally between thegrandparents
All to be shared equally among the unclesand aunties
No spouse，children, parents,       siblings,siblings'      children,grandparents, unclesor aunties

cells.value: Half to spouse and the other half to bedivided equally among the children. If thechildren of the intestate has passed away,then the portion goes to the children'sdescendants in equal proportions

All to be shared equally among siblings. If any sibling has already passed away, thenthe portion goestothesibling'sdescendants in equal proportions

narrative: Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only their spouse, the entire estate goes to the spouse.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and children, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally among the children, with the children's descendants inheriting if a child has passed away.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only children, the entire estate is divided equally among the children, with the children's descendants inheriting if a child has passed away.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and parents only, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally between the parents.
Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only parents, the entire estate is divided equally between the parents.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind siblings and their children only, the entire estate is shared equally among the siblings, with the siblings' descendants inheriting if a sibling has passed away.
Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only grandparents, the entire estate is shared equally between the grandparents.
Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only uncles and/or aunties, the entire estate is shared equally among the uncles and aunties.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind no spouse, children, parents, siblings, siblings' children, grandparents, uncles, or aunties, the entire estate goes to the government.

dimensions.row: The rows represent different types of insurance policies or categories of policy owners.
dimensions.col: The columns represent whether Trust Nomination and Revocable Nomination are allowed for each policy type.
dimensions.value: The values indicate whether Trust Nomination and Revocable Nomination are permitted, with specific conditions noted.

raw_grid: Aims Of Nomination               To provide policy owners with greater choice and flexibility in determining theFramework                           manner in which the proceeds from their insurance policies are to be disbursecTo accord adequate financial protection to the named beneficiaries.To offer greater clarity and certainty in respect of nominations of beneficiaries to insurance policy proceeds.
Types Of Nominations Allowed Under VariousTypes Of Policies
Accident and Health Policy with Death Benefits

Group insurance covers a group of people under one master policy. Typically, a groupinsurance policy is bought by an organisation such as an employer for thebenefit of its individual employees. The insurer issues the master policy contract tothe organisation. Thus, the organisation becomes the policy owner. The individual employee does not have a policy with the insurer,but the benefits ofthe master policy wil go to him as a form of employee benefit. As the policy owner(the organisation) is also not the life insured in such a case, insurance nomination is not allowed for any group insurance policy.

When the policy owner makes a trust nomination at the time of buying a policy or at any time after the policy is issued, he will lose all rights to the ownership of the policy. This means that al proceeds (living benefits and death benefits) from thepolicy now belong to the nominees whom he named. While he is still obliged to paythe premiums for the policy,allthe benefits of the policy belong to the nominees. Only a policy owner who is at least 18 years old can make a trust nomination.

When a policy owner makes a revocable nomination, he willcontinue to retain fullrights and ownership over the policy. This means that he can change or revoke a nomination at any time, without needing the written consent of the nominee or nominees. Only death benefits from the policy willbe payable to the nominees. Allliving benefits will be paid to the policy owner. Hence, revocable nominations offer the benefit of maximum flexibility to the policy owner.
hierarchical_json.spanning_title: Aims Of Nomination               To provide policy owners with greater choice and flexibility in determining theFramework                           manner in which the proceeds from their insurance policies are to be disbursecTo accord adequate financial protection to the named beneficiaries.To offer greater clarity and certainty in respect of nominations of beneficiaries to insurance policy proceeds.

narrative: The Aims Of Nomination Framework are to provide policy owners with greater choice and flexibility in determining the manner in which the proceeds from their insurance policies are to be disbursed, to accord adequate financial protection to the named beneficiaries, and to offer greater clarity and certainty in respect of nominations of beneficiaries to insurance policy proceeds.
For Life Policy, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For Accident and Health Policy with Death Benefits, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For CPFIS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For DPS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For SRS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For Muslim Policy owner, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed (subject to Muslim Law).
For ElderShield Supplementary Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For Integrated Shield Plan, Trust Nomination is not allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed*.

Group insurance covers a group of people under one master policy, typically bought by an organisation for its employees; as the organisation is the policy owner and not the life insured, insurance nomination is not allowed for any group insurance policy.

When a policy owner makes a trust nomination, they lose all rights to the ownership of the policy; all proceeds belong to the nominees, although the policy owner remains obliged to pay premiums, and only a policy owner who is at least 18 years old can make a trust nomination.

When a policy owner makes a revocable nomination, they retain full rights and ownership over the policy, allowing them to change or revoke a nomination at any time without consent; only death benefits are payable to the nominees, while all living benefits are paid to the policy owner, offering maximum flexibility.

raw_grid: Can the policy owner retain control over the policy for as long as he is alive?
Who gets the policy proceeds? - Living benefits- Death benefits
Nominees get both types of benefits.
Living benefits are paid to the policy owner. Nominees only get the death benefits.
Can the policyowner change hisnomination byhimself at any time?
No The nomination canbe revoked only if any trustee who is not the policy owner gives consent; or if all the nominees give writtenconsent.
Can the policy owner name one or more nominees who arenot his spouse orchildren
No Only spouse and / or children can benominated.

Can the policy ownermake a Will after having made anearlier nomination?
The Will has no impact on the earlier trust

The earlier revocablenomination is revokedby the Will, provided that the Wili contains the information as prescribed in the Insurance (Nomination of Beneficiaries) Regulations2009.Note: The insurer wil pay according to thelatest properly executed instrument (be it arevocable nomination or a Will) that is known to the insurer at the time of the policy owner's death.
the policy no longer belongs to the policyowner. As such, he cannot give it awayunder his Will, as he can give away only property under theWill which still belongs to him.

narrative: The table compares Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination and Revocable Nomination for insurance policies, detailing considerations for the policy owner.
For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot retain control over the policy for as long as he is alive.
For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can retain control over the policy for as long as he is alive.
For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, nominees get both living benefits and death benefits.
For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, living benefits are paid to the policy owner, and nominees only get the death benefits.

For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot change the nomination by himself at any time; the nomination can be revoked only if any trustee who is not the policy owner gives consent, or if all the nominees give written consent.
For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can change his nomination by himself at any time.
For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot name one or more nominees who are not his spouse or children; only spouse and/or children can be nominated.

For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can name one or more nominees who are not his spouse or children; any legal entity, including spouse and/or children, can be nominated, but the nomination of an animal is invalid.
For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, a Will has no impact on the earlier trust.

For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the earlier revocable nomination is revoked by the Will, provided that the Will contains the information as prescribed in the Insurance (Nomination of Beneficiaries) Regulations 2009.
The insurer will pay according to the latest properly executed instrument (be it a revocable nomination or a Will) that is known to the insurer at the time of the policy owner's death.
Once a trust nomination is made for an insurance policy, the policy no longer belongs to the policy owner.
Once a trust nomination is made for an insurance policy, the policy owner cannot give it away under his Will, as he can give away only property under the Will which still belongs to him.

raw_grid: Intestate Succession Act 1967 Rules of Distribution of

cells.value: Intestate Succession Act 1967 Rules of Distribution of

narrative: The Intestate Succession Act 1967 provides rules for the distribution of intestate assets.
The Intestate Succession Act 1967 outlines the rules of distribution of intestate assets.

dimensions.row: The Intestate Succession Act 1967 rules for property distribution.
dimensions.col: The beneficiaries of the deceased's estate.
dimensions.value: The distribution of the estate among the beneficiaries.

raw_grid: Half to spouse and the other half to bedivided equally among the children. If the children of the intestate has passed away, then the portion goes to the children'sdescendants in equal proportions
All to be divided equally among the children. If the children of the intestate have passed away, then the portion goes to the children'sdescendants in equal proportions
Half to spouse and the other half to be divided equally between the parents
All to be shared equally among siblings. If any sibling has already passed away, then the portion goes to the sibling's descendants in equal proportions
All to be shared equally between the grandparents
All to be shared equally among the uncles and aunties
No spouse, children, parents, siblings, siblings' children, grandparents, unclesor aunties

cells.value: Half to spouse and the other half to bedivided equally among the children. If the children of the intestate has passed away, then the portion goes to the children'sdescendants in equal proportions
All to be divided equally among the children. If the children of the intestate have passed away, then the portion goes to the children'sdescendants in equal proportions
Half to spouse and the other half to be divided equally between the parents
All to be shared equally among siblings. If any sibling has already passed away, then the portion goes to the sibling's descendants in equal proportions
All to be shared equally between the grandparents
All to be shared equally among the uncles and aunties
No spouse, children, parents, siblings, siblings' children, grandparents, unclesor aunties

narrative: Under Rule 1 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only a spouse, the entire estate goes to the spouse.

Under Rule 2 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and children, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally among the children, with the portion going to deceased children's descendants.

Under Rule 3 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only children, the entire estate is divided equally among the children, with the portion going to deceased children's descendants.

Under Rule 4 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and parents, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally between the parents.
Under Rule 5 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only parents, the entire estate is divided equally between the parents.

Under Rule 6 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only siblings and their children, the entire estate is shared equally among the siblings, with the portion going to deceased siblings' descendants.
Under Rule 7 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only grandparents, the entire estate is shared equally between the grandparents.
Under Rule 8 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only uncles and/or aunties, the entire estate is shared equally among the uncles and aunties.

Under Rule 9 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away with no spouse, children, parents, siblings, siblings' children, grandparents, uncles, or aunties, the entire estate goes to the government.

Typically, a groupinsurance policy is bought by an organisation such as an employer for thebenefit of its individual employees. The insurer issues the master policy contract tothe organisation. As the policy owner(the organisation) is also not the life insured in such a case, insurance nomination is not allowed for any group insurance policy.", "", "", ""], ["Group Insurance", "", "", "", "", ""], ["Trust Nomination", "When the policy owner makes a trust nomination at the time of buying a policy or at any time after the policy is issued, he will lose all rights to the ownership of the policy. Only a policy owner who is at least 18 years old can make a trust nomination.", "", "", "", ""], ["Revocable Nomination", "When a policy owner makes a revocable nomination, he willcontinue to retain fullrights and ownership over the policy. This means that he can change or revoke a nomination at any time, without needing the written consent of the nominee or nominees. Only death benefits from the policy willbe payable to the nominees. Allliving benefits will be paid to the policy owner. Hence, revocable nominations offer the benefit of maximum flexibility to the policy owner.", "", "", "", ""]], "spanning_title": "Aims Of Nomination               To provide policy owners with greater choice and flexibility in determining theFramework                           manner in which the proceeds from their insurance policies are to be disbursecTo accord adequate financial protection to the named beneficiaries.To offer greater clarity and certainty in respect of nominations of beneficiaries to insurance policy proceeds."}

table narrative: ["The Aims Of Nomination Framework are to provide policy owners with greater choice and flexibility in determining the manner in which the proceeds from their insurance policies are to be disbursed, to accord adequate financial protection to the named beneficiaries, and to offer greater clarity and certainty in respect of nominations of beneficiaries to insurance policy proceeds.", "For Life Policy, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed.", "For Accident and Health Policy with Death Benefits, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed.", "For CPFIS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.", "For DPS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.", "For SRS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.", "For Muslim Policy owner, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed (subject to Muslim Law).", "For ElderShield Supplementary Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.", "For Integrated Shield Plan, Trust Nomination is not allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed*.", "Group insurance covers a group of people under one master policy, typically bought by an organisation for its employees; as the organisation is the policy owner and not the life insured, insurance nomination is not allowed for any group insurance policy.", "When a policy owner makes a trust nomination, they lose all rights to the ownership of the policy; all proceeds belong to the nominees, although the policy owner remains obliged to pay premiums, and only a policy owner who is at least 18 years old can make a trust nomination.", "When a policy owner makes a revocable nomination, they retain full rights and ownership over the policy, allowing them to change or revoke a nomination at any time without consent; only death benefits are payable to the nominees, while all living benefits are paid to the policy owner, offering maximum flexibility."]

table narrative: ["The table compares Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination and Revocable Nomination for insurance policies, detailing considerations for the policy owner.", "For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot retain control over the policy for as long as he is alive.", "For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can retain control over the policy for as long as he is alive.", "For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, nominees get both living benefits and death benefits.", "For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, living benefits are paid to the policy owner, and nominees only get the death benefits.", "For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot change the nomination by himself at any time; the nomination can be revoked only if any trustee who is not the policy owner gives consent, or if all the nominees give written consent.", "For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can change his nomination by himself at any time.", "For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot name one or more nominees who are not his spouse or children; only spouse and/or children can be nominated.", "For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can name one or more nominees who are not his spouse or children; any legal entity, including spouse and/or children, can be nominated, but the nomination of an animal is invalid.", "For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, a Will has no impact on the earlier trust.", "For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the earlier revocable nomination is revoked by the Will, provided that the Will contains the information as prescribed in the Insurance (Nomination of Beneficiaries) Regulations 2009.", "The insurer will pay according to the latest properly executed instrument (be it a revocable nomination or a Will) that is known to the insurer at the time of the policy owner's death.", "Once a trust nomination is made for an insurance policy, the policy no longer belongs to the policy owner.", "Once a trust nomination is made for an insurance policy, the policy owner cannot give it away under his Will, as he can give away only property under the Will which still belongs to him."]

dimensions.row: Describes the individuals left behind by the deceased.
dimensions.col: Describes how the deceased's estate is distributed.
dimensions.value: Details the distribution rules for the deceased's estate.

narrative: Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only their spouse, the entire estate goes to the spouse.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and children, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally among the children, with the children's descendants inheriting if a child has passed away.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only children, the entire estate is divided equally among the children, with the children's descendants inheriting if a child has passed away.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and parents only, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally between the parents.
Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only parents, the entire estate is divided equally between the parents.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind siblings and their children only, the entire estate is shared equally among the siblings, with the siblings' descendants inheriting if a sibling has passed away.
Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only grandparents, the entire estate is shared equally between the grandparents.
Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only uncles and/or aunties, the entire estate is shared equally among the uncles and aunties.

Under the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind no spouse, children, parents, siblings, siblings' children, grandparents, uncles, or aunties, the entire estate goes to the government.

dimensions.row: The rows represent different types of insurance policies or categories of policy owners.
dimensions.col: The columns represent whether Trust Nomination and Revocable Nomination are allowed for each policy type.
dimensions.value: The values indicate whether Trust Nomination and Revocable Nomination are permitted, with specific conditions noted.

raw_grid: Aims Of Nomination               To provide policy owners with greater choice and flexibility in determining theFramework                           manner in which the proceeds from their insurance policies are to be disbursecTo accord adequate financial protection to the named beneficiaries.To offer greater clarity and certainty in respect of nominations of beneficiaries to insurance policy proceeds.
Types Of Nominations Allowed Under VariousTypes Of Policies
Accident and Health Policy with Death Benefits

Group insurance covers a group of people under one master policy. Typically, a groupinsurance policy is bought by an organisation such as an employer for thebenefit of its individual employees. The insurer issues the master policy contract tothe organisation. Thus, the organisation becomes the policy owner. The individual employee does not have a policy with the insurer,but the benefits ofthe master policy wil go to him as a form of employee benefit. As the policy owner(the organisation) is also not the life insured in such a case, insurance nomination is not allowed for any group insurance policy.

When the policy owner makes a trust nomination at the time of buying a policy or at any time after the policy is issued, he will lose all rights to the ownership of the policy. This means that al proceeds (living benefits and death benefits) from thepolicy now belong to the nominees whom he named. While he is still obliged to paythe premiums for the policy,allthe benefits of the policy belong to the nominees. Only a policy owner who is at least 18 years old can make a trust nomination.

When a policy owner makes a revocable nomination, he willcontinue to retain fullrights and ownership over the policy. This means that he can change or revoke a nomination at any time, without needing the written consent of the nominee or nominees. Only death benefits from the policy willbe payable to the nominees. Allliving benefits will be paid to the policy owner. Hence, revocable nominations offer the benefit of maximum flexibility to the policy owner.
hierarchical_json.spanning_title: Aims Of Nomination               To provide policy owners with greater choice and flexibility in determining theFramework                           manner in which the proceeds from their insurance policies are to be disbursecTo accord adequate financial protection to the named beneficiaries.To offer greater clarity and certainty in respect of nominations of beneficiaries to insurance policy proceeds.

narrative: The Aims Of Nomination Framework are to provide policy owners with greater choice and flexibility in determining the manner in which the proceeds from their insurance policies are to be disbursed, to accord adequate financial protection to the named beneficiaries, and to offer greater clarity and certainty in respect of nominations of beneficiaries to insurance policy proceeds.
For Life Policy, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For Accident and Health Policy with Death Benefits, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For CPFIS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For DPS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For SRS Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For Muslim Policy owner, Trust Nomination is allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed (subject to Muslim Law).
For ElderShield Supplementary Policy, Trust Nomination is not allowed but Revocable Nomination is allowed.
For Integrated Shield Plan, Trust Nomination is not allowed and Revocable Nomination is allowed*.

Group insurance covers a group of people under one master policy, typically bought by an organisation for its employees; as the organisation is the policy owner and not the life insured, insurance nomination is not allowed for any group insurance policy.

When a policy owner makes a trust nomination, they lose all rights to the ownership of the policy; all proceeds belong to the nominees, although the policy owner remains obliged to pay premiums, and only a policy owner who is at least 18 years old can make a trust nomination.

When a policy owner makes a revocable nomination, they retain full rights and ownership over the policy, allowing them to change or revoke a nomination at any time without consent; only death benefits are payable to the nominees, while all living benefits are paid to the policy owner, offering maximum flexibility.

narrative: The table compares Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination and Revocable Nomination for insurance policies, detailing considerations for the policy owner.
For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot retain control over the policy for as long as he is alive.
For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can retain control over the policy for as long as he is alive.
For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, nominees get both living benefits and death benefits.
For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, living benefits are paid to the policy owner, and nominees only get the death benefits.

For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot change the nomination by himself at any time; the nomination can be revoked only if any trustee who is not the policy owner gives consent, or if all the nominees give written consent.
For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can change his nomination by himself at any time.
For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, the policy owner cannot name one or more nominees who are not his spouse or children; only spouse and/or children can be nominated.

For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the policy owner can name one or more nominees who are not his spouse or children; any legal entity, including spouse and/or children, can be nominated, but the nomination of an animal is invalid.
For insurance policies, under a Trust (Irrevocable) Nomination, a Will has no impact on the earlier trust.

For insurance policies, under a Revocable Nomination, the earlier revocable nomination is revoked by the Will, provided that the Will contains the information as prescribed in the Insurance (Nomination of Beneficiaries) Regulations 2009.
The insurer will pay according to the latest properly executed instrument (be it a revocable nomination or a Will) that is known to the insurer at the time of the policy owner's death.
Once a trust nomination is made for an insurance policy, the policy no longer belongs to the policy owner.
Once a trust nomination is made for an insurance policy, the policy owner cannot give it away under his Will, as he can give away only property under the Will which still belongs to him.

narrative: Under Rule 1 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only a spouse, the entire estate goes to the spouse.

Under Rule 2 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and children, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally among the children, with the portion going to deceased children's descendants.

Under Rule 3 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only children, the entire estate is divided equally among the children, with the portion going to deceased children's descendants.

Under Rule 4 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and parents, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally between the parents.
Under Rule 5 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only parents, the entire estate is divided equally between the parents.

Under Rule 6 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only siblings and their children, the entire estate is shared equally among the siblings, with the portion going to deceased siblings' descendants.
Under Rule 7 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only grandparents, the entire estate is shared equally between the grandparents.
Under Rule 8 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only uncles and/or aunties, the entire estate is shared equally among the uncles and aunties.

Under Rule 9 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away with no spouse, children, parents, siblings, siblings' children, grandparents, uncles, or aunties, the entire estate goes to the government.

context: This chunk defines key legal terms related to estate planning, including Wills, Probate, Executors, and different types of Trusts (Express, Implied/Resulting, Constructive), along with the benefits of trusts. It also touches upon the Intestate Succession Act and the distribution rules for intestate assets.
text:
<context>This chunk defines key legal terms related to estate planning, including Wills, Probate, Executors, and different types of Trusts (Express, Implied/Resulting, Constructive), along with the benefits of trusts. It also touches upon the Intestate Succession Act and the distribution rules for intestate assets.</context>
# Chapter summary:

- Definition or Explanation: Wills
A Will is basically a legal document that is executed by an individual (called the"testator"), which sets out how his estate should be managed and the assetsdistributed when he should pass away. The Will takes effect only on the death of the testator. He is free to change the Willas and when he likes, and as many times as he wants, up until the point of death.

- Definition or Explanation: Grant Of Probate
A grant of probate must be obtained from the Court to enable the executor to administer the deceased's estate,according to the directions as contained in hisWill.

- Definition or Explanation: Executor
An executor is the person nominated to administer the deceased's estate according to the Will If the executor is a female, she is called an executrix. The executor canbe a beneficiary under the Willor some disinterested party, e.g. a solicitor,a bankeror friends, etc.

- Definition or Explanation: Trust
A trust is in essence an agreement between a settlor, the person who settles property into the trust, and the trustee,the person to whom the property is transferred. The trust property would be heid by trustees "in trust for" thebeneficiaries. The beneficiaries of the trust are, therefore, the people who willreceive the benefit of the income and / or capital of the trust fund.

- Definition or Explanation: Express Trust
We are using the term "express trusts" in a wide sense to cover situations, where there is an express intention to create a trust over a life policy (which intention is not required for a statutory trust). In strict law, there would be three certaintieswhich are required, namely, certainty of words, certainty of subject matter andcertainty of objects.In simple English,the intention to create a trust should be clear, and the beneficiaries should be ascertainable.

- Definition or Explanation: Implied Or Resulting Trust
A trust may be implied or may "result" in certain situations with regard to lifepolicies.

# Chapter summary:

- Definition or Explanation: Benefits of Trusts
Distribution: The trust deed provides the distribution of the assets held by the trustees in accordance with the wishes of the settor and at the time that he intends the beneficiaries to benefit.Avoiding probate: The settlor can plan for the smooth transfer of assets to his next of kin, without going through costly probate procedures, especially if the settlor has assets in various countries registered in his name. Confidentiality: A trust will not expose your estate to the public scrutiny that probate procedures will. Succession: A trust can also be a good vehicle to plan for the succession tobusiness assets Estate duty: A trust may also help to minimise estate duty liability exposure invarious jurisdictions. Asset protection: Depending on how a trust is structured, it could protect the assets of the trust from future creditors. tlorh Confident

- Definition or Explanation: Intestate Succession Act1967
The Intestate Succession Act 1967 provides rules for the transfer of property. This Act spells out the rules for distributing the deceased's property to the spouse,children and relatives. The distribution rules under the Act are fixed.

table narrative: ["Under Rule 1 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only a spouse, the entire estate goes to the spouse.", "Under Rule 2 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and children, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally among the children, with the portion going to deceased children's descendants.", "Under Rule 3 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only children, the entire estate is divided equally among the children, with the portion going to deceased children's descendants.", "Under Rule 4 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind a spouse and parents, half of the estate goes to the spouse and the other half is divided equally between the parents.", "Under Rule 5 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only parents, the entire estate is divided equally between the parents.", "Under Rule 6 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only siblings and their children, the entire estate is shared equally among the siblings, with the portion going to deceased siblings' descendants.", "Under Rule 7 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only grandparents, the entire estate is shared equally between the grandparents.", "Under Rule 8 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away leaving behind only uncles and/or aunties, the entire estate is shared equally among the uncles and aunties.", "Under Rule 9 of the Intestate Succession Act 1967, if a person passes away with no spouse, children, parents, siblings, siblings' children, grandparents, uncles, or aunties, the entire estate goes to the government."]
